visible_agent_inputs.question: What year was the crime novel written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle written, featuring Sherlock Holmes?
visible_agent_inputs.summary_1:
Primary topics: Sherlock Holmes novels by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle

- Facts from passages:
  - "The Sign of the Four (1890), also called The Sign of Four" — "is the second novel featuring Sherlock Holmes written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle."
  - "Doyle wrote four novels and 56 stories" (starring the fictional detective).
  - "The Hound of the Baskervilles" — "is the third of four Sherlock Holmes novels written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle." (no year given in passages)

- Ambiguities / missing facts:
  - The query's phrase "the crime novel" is ambiguous — candidate novels in the passages: "The Sign of the Four (1890)" and "The Hound of the Baskervilles" (ambiguous title — no year present).
  - Missing fact: publication year for "The Hound of the Baskervilles" is not provided in the supplied passages; bridge entity = "The Hound of the Baskervilles" page.

- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. Fetch page "The Sign of the Four" — inspect lede/infobox and "Publication" or "Background" sections to confirm year and genre.
  2. Fetch page "The Hound of the Baskervilles" — inspect lede/infobox and "Publication" or "History" sections to obtain its publication year.
  3. Fetch "Canon of Sherlock Holmes" or "List of Sherlock Holmes novels" — lede/infobox to list all four novels and their publication years to resolve which novel the user means.

current_output.query: The Hound of the Baskervilles publication year

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.

retrieved_doc_snippets: The Hound of the Baskervilles (disambiguation) | The Hound of the Baskervilles is the third of four Sherlock Holmes novels written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle.

The Hound of the Baskervilles | The Hound of the Baskervilles is the third of the crime novels written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle featuring the detective Sherlock Holmes. Originally serialised in "The Strand Magazine" from August 1901 to April 1902, it is set largely on Dartmoor in Devon in England's West Country and tells the story of an attempted murder inspired by the legend of a fearsome, diabolical hound of supernatural origin. Sherlock Holmes and his companion Dr. Watson investigate the cas ...[truncated]

Sherlock Holmes and the Baskerville Curse | Sherlock Holmes and the Baskerville Curse is a 1983 Australian film directed by Eddie Graham. It is an adaptation of Sir Arthur Conan Doyle's novel "The Hound of the Baskervilles", the third of his novels featuring Sherlock Holmes and Dr. John Watson.

The Hounds of Baskerville | "The Hounds of Baskerville" is the second episode of the second series of the BBC crime drama series "Sherlock", which follows the modern-day adventures of Sherlock Holmes, and was first broadcast by BBC One on 8 January 2012. It was written by co-creator Mark Gatiss, who also portrays Mycroft Holmes, Sherlock's brother in the series, and was directed by Paul McGuigan. The episode is a contemporary adaptation of "The Hound of the Baskervilles", one of Sir Arthur Conan ...[truncated]

The Hound of the Baskervilles (1929 film) | (German: Der Hund von Baskerville) is a 1929 German silent mystery film directed by Richard Oswald and starring Carlyle Blackwell, Alexander Murski, Livio Pavanelli. The film is an adaptation of the Sherlock Holmes novel "The Hound of the Baskervilles" by Arthur Conan Doyle. It was the last Sherlock Holmes adaptation in the silent film era.

The Hound of the Baskervilles (1972 film) | The Hound of the Baskervilles (1972) is an American television film directed by Barry Crane and starring Stewart Granger as Sherlock Holmes and Bernard Fox as Doctor Watson. The movie is based on Arthur Conan Doyle's Sherlock Holmes novel "The Hound of the Baskervilles".

The Hound of the Baskervilles (1959 film) | The Hound of the Baskervilles is a 1959 British gothic horror and mystery film, directed by Terence Fisher and produced by Hammer Film Productions. It is based on the novel of the same name by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle. It stars Peter Cushing as Sherlock Holmes, Sir Christopher Lee as Sir Henry Baskerville and André Morell as Doctor Watson.
target_state.summary_2: {"summary":"The Sign of the Four (1890), also called The Sign of Four — is the second novel featuring Sherlock Holmes written by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle. The Hound of the Baskervilles was originally serialised in The Strand Magazine from August 1901 to April 1902."}
target_state.answer:
The summaries give two possibilities:
- The Sign of the Four — 1890.
- The Hound of the Baskervilles — serialised August 1901 to April 1902 (1901–1902).

Which novel did you mean?
